coredata.dc:title: Carbide-directed enhancement of electrochemical hydrogen evolution reaction on tungsten carbide–oxide heterostructure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overpotentials as a function of tungsten carbide content (%) determined from deconvolution of (a) W 4f and (b) C 1s XPS spectra.

head.ce:title: Carbide-directed enhancement of electrochemical hydrogen evolution reaction on tungsten carbide–oxide heterostructure

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Research Center
Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST)

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the challenge of climate change, there is an ever-increasing desire for sustainable energy, which calls for renewable energy sources to replace fossil fuel energy systems. However, as renewable energy is intermittent causing a mismatch between supply and demand, it is necessary to store surplus energy into energy carriers [1]. Due to its high gravimetric energy density and sustainable properties, hydrogen is a promising candidate as an energy carrier [2]. Current hydrogen production processes rely on steam-natural gas reforming and coal gasification based on fossil fuels, which emit by-products such as CO, CO2, and other greenhouse gases that affect the environment [3,4]. On the contrary, water electrolysis produces eco-friendly and high-purity hydrogen without generating harzardous by-products or carbon emissions other than hydrogen and oxygen [5,6]. Particularly, hydrogen production in acidic media, such as in polymer electrolyte membrane water electrolysis (PEMWE) has the advantages of high current density, high pressure compatibility, and simplicity compared to alkaline water electrolysis [5]. To boost the efficiency of PEMWE, Pt is commonly employed as the electrocatalyst for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) because of its high electrocatalytic performance [7,8]. However, the high cost and scarcity of Pt make it unsuitable for massive applications, which in turn have prompted the development of non-precious metal-based HER catalysts [9,10].

Tungsten carbide has long been advocated as potential alternative to Pt catalyst for the HER in acidic environment because of its Pt-like electronic structure and outstanding chemical/electrochemical stability [11-15]. A recent theoretical calculation demonstrated that the introduction of surface tungsten oxide can further improve the HER activity of tungsten carbide due to electronic coupling within the oxide/carbide heterojunction [16]. Recently, such transition metal-based carbide–oxide heterostructures have shown improved HER activity compared to their single counterparts owing to interfacial synergistic effect and electronic modification [16-18]. In spite of the advantages, only a few carbide-oxide heterostructures including a-WO x /WC [16], and Mo2C-MoO x
[17], have been adopted as the HER catalysts in acid due to difficulties in preparation of catalyst with a high density of heterointerfaces. Typical WC fabrication requires a complex and harsh reaction environment: i) annealing of WO3 (or W metal) with carbon powder at high temperature (1400 – 1600 °C), and (ii) reductive carbonization under mixed gaseous CH4/CO and H2 at a high temperature (900 – 1200 °C) [19,20]. This approach typically causes sintering and collapse of the heterostructure during synthesis [21,22]. To overcome this issue, several alternative methods have been developed, such as ball milling [23], microwave heating [24], plasma treatment [14,25], and chemical vapor deposition [20,26]. However, these processes are still not effective for producing abundant carbide-oxide heterointerfaces. Furthermore, these as-reported tungsten carbides cannot be as efficient as Pt catalysts [27,28].

Here, nano-sized carbon dots (CDs, ∼2 nm in diameter) were adopted as carbon sources and dispersed on tungsten oxide nanorods, allowing abundant nucleation sites for oxide-to-carbide conversion. This approach suggests a new facile way for generating high density of carbide-oxide heterointerfaces, which are active for the HER in acid. Indeed, optimization of the synthesis condition produced tungsten oxide-carbide nanorods decorated with CDs (WO(3−
x
) − WC y /CDs), which exhibited a remarkable HER performance (overpotential (η) of 65 mV at − 10 mA cm−2 with a Tafel slope of 50 mV dec−1). The HER performance exceeds those of state-of-the-art tungsten carbide-based catalysts. Combined analysis of the X-ray spectroscopic and electrochemical results established a correlation between the composition and HER activity, suggesting an optimal W–C ratio in the tungsten carbide-oxide heterostructure. DFT calculations showed that incorporation of C into the WO3-
x cluster improved the HER activity, supporting the experimental results. Furthermore, a single-cell test demonstrated its applicability to a commercial PEMWE, showing high HER performance close to that of the benchmark catalyst of Pt. This finding can provide a rational guideline for designing active HER catalysts and accelerate the commercialization of non-precious catalyst-based PEMWE.

The CDs were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method following a previous report with some modifications [29]. Briefly, 0.28 mmol of uric acid (UA Sigma-Aldrich, 99 %) was dissolved in 40 mL of deionized (DI) water. After ultrasonication for 1 h, 8.8 mmol of glucose was added to the mixture solution under vigorous stirring to obtain a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, the slurry was placed in a Teflon-stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the as-obtained solution was centrifuged (8000 rpm) and purified using a dialysis tube to eliminate all residual chemicals.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Preparation of WO(3
−
x) − WC y /CDs

ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesis of WO3−
x followed a process similar to that for WO(3−
x
) − WC y /CDs without the addition of a CDs solution.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were taken on a field-emission electron microscope (JEM–F200, JEOL) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE–SEM) (Teneo VS, FEI). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a high-power X-ray diffractometer (D8 ADVANCE, Bruker) equipped with Cu Kα radiation and operated at 40 kV and 40 mA. The chemical states of the samples were analyzed using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Nexsa, ThermoFisher Scientific) equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV). Curve fitting of the XPS spectra was performed using XPSpeak41 software. The baseline of the XPS spectra was corrected using a Shirley-type background with a zero slope. The full-width-at-half-maximum (FWHM) values of the main peaks were constrained to 2.0 eV.

All electrochemical experiments were conducted in a three-electrode system using an Autolab PGSTAT302N potentiostat at room temperature. A graphite rod and Ag/AgCl electrode (3 M KCl) were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. A rotating disk electrode (RDE) containing glassy carbon (GC) as the central disk (5 mm diameter, 0.196 cm2 area) was used as the working electrode. To prepare the catalyst ink, 5 mg of the catalyst was dissolved in a mixture solution of 200 μL of DI water, 25 μL of Nafion® (75 μL, 5 wt% in water and isopropanol, Sigma-Aldrich), and 975 μL of isopropanol (Acros Organic, 99.5 %), followed by sonication for 30 min to produce a homogeneous slurry. Then, 20 μL of the ink dispersion was dropped onto the surface of the GC electrode and dried. The mass loading was estimated to be approximately 0.425 mg cm−2. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was carried out in 0.5 M H2SO4 purged with Ar for electrochemical cleaning; the process involved 50 cycles in a potential range of 0.05 to 1.2 V (vs. reversible hydrogen electrode, RHE) at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was obtained from 0.1 to −0.6 V (vs. RHE) in H2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 and a rotation speed of 1600 rpm after 50 CVs. The LSV was measured three times for each sample, and averaged data were used. In this study, all potential values were plotted against RHE using the following equation: ERHE = EAg/AgCl + Eo Ag/AgCl + 0.059·pH. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed using the same electrochemical setup. The Nyquist plots were measured in the frequency range of 10 kHz to 1 Hz at an overpotential of 600 mV with an AC voltage amplitude of 10 mV. The obtained impedance spectra were fitted using Zview software to a simplified equivalent Randles circuit model. The series resistances (R s ) and charge-transfer resistances (R ct ) of the samples are summarized in Table S6. All the LSV curves reported here were plotted after compensating for the Ohmic drops with the series resistance acquired from the EIS measurements. A stability cycling test was conducted using CV with 1000 cycles in the potential range of −0.5 to 0.2 V (vs. RHE) at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The electrochemical surface areas (ECSA) were calculated from the electrochemical double-layer capacitance (C dl) [30,31]. C dl was determined from the CV measured in the non-Faradaic potential region at various scan rates. The potential range is typically centered at open-circuit potential (OCP) with a potential window of 0.1 V, all measured currents are assumed to be related to double-layer charging. The charging current (ic ) is equal to the product of C dl and scan rate (ν) using the following equation: ic = ν⋅C dl. Thus, C dl is the slope obtained from the plot of ic as a function of ν. The ECSA of a catalyst is determined by dividing C dl by the specific capacitance of the sample (C s) using the following equation: ECSA = C dl/C s. In this study, based on typical reports, a general specific capacitance (C s) of 0.035 mF cm−2 was used for the calculation [31,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Membrane electrode assembly and single-cell test
ce:section.ce:para: All single-cell tests were performed by flowing water at a flow rate of 3.75 mL min−1 at 80 °C. The electrode was activated at 1.55 V and 80 °C for 30 min. The LSV measurements were taken in the potential range of 1.2–2.2 V at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. The EIS measurements were conducted in a frequency range of 50 kHz to 50 mHz at a potential of 1.5 or 1.9 V. The stability test was performed with chronopotentiometry (CP) measurements at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. All electrochemical tests were performed using an HCP-803 potentiostat (BioLogic, France).

The O 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2c) can be resolved into six peaks centered at around 530.1, 531.0, 531.9, 532.4, 533.4 eV, and 535.0 eV, corresponding to W−O, O vacancy, C=O, adsorbed O, C−O bonding, and O−F x species, respectively [54,55]. The C 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2d) can be fitted with five peaks centered at approximately 283.2, 284.9, 286.0, 286.9, and 289.1 eV, which were assigned to W−C, C−C, C−O, C=N, and C=O species, respectively [53,56]. Quantification of the peak areas revealed that the relative peak area of W−C reached a maximum at a hydrothermal temperature of 180 °C, in accordance with the W 4f XPS spectra (Table S3). Furthermore, the XPS spectra of the samples (6, 12, and 24 h) were also analyzed (Fig. S12, Table S2, Table S4). The W−C portion obtained in both the W 4f and C 1s spectra were the highest in the sample with a reaction time of 12 h among the comparison samples. Overall, the XPS results are in good agreement with the XRD results.

To illustrate their synergy, Fig. S13 presents the HER performances of bare CDs, WO3−
x , and WO(3−
x
) − WC y /CDs. CDs and WO3−
x showed poor catalytic activities in the HER. Simultaneously, after introduction of CDs as C sources to form tungsten carbide as well as conductive matrix, the WO(3−
x
) − WC y /CDs required a very low overpotential (65 mV) than that of bare WO3−
x (412 mV) to achieve a current density of − 10 mA cm−2. Additionally, the Tafel slope of WO(3−
x
) − WC y /CDs (50 mV dec−1) was much lower than that of WO3−
x (188 mV dec−1). The activity enhancement mainly originates from the synergistic effect between WO3−
x , WC x , and CDs, in which nano-sized CDs serve as conductive matrix to improve electron transfer kinetics, and increase interfacial reaction sites while tungsten carbide/oxide act as primary active components.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correlation between the amount of tungsten carbide and HER activity
ce:section.ce:para: According to the XRD analysis (Fig. S20d), no peaks related to graphitic carbon were observed above 850 °C owing to the decomposition of CDs. As the CDs play important roles as carbide sources and conductive matrix for the dispersion of electroactive phases, such high temperature treatment could deteriorate the HER activity. Expectedly, the HER activities of the composites calcined above 850 °C were lower than those of the catalyst calcined at 850 °C (Fig. S21). Furthermore, at temperatures below 850 °C, catalytically less active species could be excessively formed resulting in poor HER performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Single Cell Test of WO(3−x) − WCy/CDs

ce:section.ce:para:
To support the experimental results, theoretical calculations were performed. HER can be carried out via a simple reaction passing through a hydrogen-adsorbed reaction intermediate, as given below [61],
(1)
∗
+
H
+
a q .
+
e
-
→
H
∗
→
∗
+
1 2
H 2
which can further be expressed as 2H* → H2, where * represents the active site of the catalyst.

Using a computational hydrogen electrode (CHE) and considering the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) as a reference electrode, the Gibbs free energy, ΔGH
* can be calculated using the following equation [62],
(2)
Δ G
H
∗
=
Δ E
H
∗
+
Δ E
ZPE
- T
Δ S
H
- n e U -
k B
T l n 10 × p H
Here, ΔEH
*, ΔΕ ZPE, and ΔS are the change in adsorption energy, zero-point energy, and entropy of adsorbed hydrogen, respectively [63,64]. The Gibbs free energy was considered under the standard conditions (i.e., pH = 0, pressure (
p
H 2
)
= 1 bar, and T = 298.15 K); therefore, the pH and applied potential (U) terms in Equation (2) were taken as zero. The effective entropy and zero-point energy corrections used for Equation (2) were 0.035 eV and −0.205 eV, respectively [61]. The surface model of WO(3-δ) was designed by introducing an oxygen vacancy on the top layer of WO3(001). To see the effect of present carbon on the HER performance of WO(3−δ) system, one oxygen atom on the top WO surface layer was replaced by a carbon atom. We denoted this doped system as WO{(3−δ)−
y
}C y (y = 1). Similar models have also been reported to study the surface heterojunction catalyst [16] and to understand the effect of dopants and oxygen vacancies on HER performance of WO3 surfaces [65]. The structural models used for the DFT investigations are presented in Fig. S24 and S25. To calculate the Gibbs free energy for HER, all the possible adsorption sites for the H-adsorbate were explored on the surface (Fig. 7 ).

For the ideal catalyst, |ΔGH
*| was assumed to be zero, while a |ΔGH
*| value close to zero was considered as a measure for the HER performance [62]. From these DFT investigations, it was found that carbon doping improves the HER activity of WO(3−δ), which supports the experimental observations. It is to be noted that the adsorption strength of H* is quite high compared to the previously reported values [16,66]. This probably originates from the simplified model used in this work,in which the oxygen atom was found to be an active site rather than either carbon or tungsten site. The large electronegativity difference between O (3.44) and H (2.20) induced the high adsorption strength of H* on the O active site. In the one carbon atom-doped system, adsorption energy was significantly reduced, showing the importance of the carbon incorporation in WO(3−δ) system for boosting HER activity. Considering that the experimental model has a higher carbon concentration on the surface than the simulated model used for the DFT calculation, the adsorption strength of H* may further decrease with increasing carbon concentration on the surface, which facilitate the subsequent HER process. In the near future, more elaborately designed theoretical model will be used to more accurately predict the HER activity of heterostructure with abundant carbide–oxide heterointerfaces.

Although, from the XRD pattern, a WO3−
x /WC y type structure was predicted in the DFT calculation, WO(3−δ) and WO{(3−δ)−
y
}C y (here y = 1) structures were considered to attain the stabilized system. Since the co-reactant CDs acted as a carbon source, the exact amount of carbide is yet to be investigated. However, it was clearly observed that the presence of carbide improved the activity by almost two-fold. Hence, this work will significantly contribute to the field of energy research.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Institutional Program of Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) and the Hydrogen Energy Innovation Technology Development Program of the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Korean government (Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT)) (No. 2019M3E6A1063674). This study was also supported by the Basic Research Laboratory Program of the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (No. 2020R1A4A4079954).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.137915.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
